lesson.title: The Definite Integral
lesson.body: The definite integral ∫_a^b f(x)dx is defined as the limit of Riemann sums and represents the net signed area between f(x) and the x-axis. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1): d/dx[∫_a^x f(t)dt] = f(x). Part 2: ∫_a^b f(x)dx = F(b) − F(a) where F' = f, allowing exact computation via antiderivatives.

rooms.question: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2 states:
rooms.latex: \int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a) \text{ where } F'= f
rooms.explanation: FTC Part 2: evaluate the antiderivative at the upper limit minus lower limit. This bridges indefinite and definite integration.

rooms.question: FTC Part 1: d/dx[∫₁ˣ t³ dt] = ?
rooms.explanation: FTC Part 1 says the derivative of ∫_a^x f(t)dt is f(x). So d/dx[∫₁ˣ t³dt] = x³.

rooms.question: Order the Riemann sum construction to define ∫_a^b f(x)dx:
items: Partition [a,b] into n subintervals of width Δx = (b−a)/n
Choose sample points xᵢ* in each subinterval
rooms.explanation: The definite integral is defined as the limit of Riemann sums. FTC then provides a shortcut for computing this limit.

rooms.question: What is ∫₋₁¹ x dx?
rooms.explanation: x is an odd function, and the interval [−1,1] is symmetric about 0. The integral of an odd function over a symmetric interval is 0. Also: F(1)−F(−1) = 1/2 − 1/2 = 0.

boss.name: The Fundamental Theorem
boss.taunt: Integration and differentiation are inverse operations. I am their bridge.

questions.question: Evaluate ∫₀^π sin(x) dx.
questions.explanation: [−cos x]₀^π = −cos(π) − (−cos(0)) = 1 + 1 = 2. This is the area under one arch of the sine curve.

questions.question: d/dx[∫_x^{x²} t² dt] = ?
questions.explanation: Chain rule on FTC: d/dx[∫_a^{g(x)} f(t)dt] = f(g(x))·g'(x). Here: (x²)²·(2x) − x²·1 = 2x⁵ − x². This is 2x³−x² only if simplified incorrectly; exact answer is 2x⁵ − x². Note: answer A listed as '(x²)²·2x − x²' is the exact form.

questions.question: The average value of f(x) = x² on [0, 3] is:
